2605.20668.title: On the limits and opportunities of AI reviewers: Reviewing the reviews of Nature-family papers with 45 expert scientists
2605.20668.paper_summary: With the advancement of AI capabilities, AI reviewers are beginning to be deployed in scientific peer review, yet their capability and credibility remain in question: many scientists simply view them as probabilistic systems without the expertise to evaluate research, while other researchers are more optimistic about their readiness without concrete evidence. Understanding what AI reviewers do well, where they fall short, and what challenges remain is essential. However, existing evaluations of AI reviewers have focused on whether their verdicts match human verdicts (e.g., score alignment, acceptance prediction), which is insufficient to characterize their capabilities and limits. In this paper, we close this gap through a large-scale expert annotation study, in which 45 domain scientists in Physical, Biological, and Health Sciences spent 469 hours rating 2,960 individual criticisms (each targeting one specific aspect of a paper) from human-written and AI-generated reviews of 82 Nature-family papers on correctness, significance, and sufficiency of evidence. On a composite of all three dimensions, a reviewing agent powered by GPT-5.2 scores above each paper's top-rated human reviewer (60.0% vs. 48.2%, p = 0.009), while all three AI reviewers (including Gemini 3.0 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5) exceed the lowest-rated human across every dimension. AI reviewers' accurate criticisms are also more often rated significant and well-evidenced, and surface a distinct 26% of issues no human raises. However, AI reviewers overlap far more than humans do (21% vs. 3% for cross-reviewer pairs), and exhibit 16 recurring weaknesses humans do not share, such as limited subfield knowledge, lack of long context management over multiple files, and overly critical stance on minor issues. Overall, our results position current AI reviewers as complements to, not substitutes for, human reviewers.
2605.20668.paper_summary_zh: 隨著人工智慧能力的進步，AI 評審者開始在科學同行評審中被部署，但它們的能力和可信度仍然受到質疑：許多科學家僅將其視為缺乏評估研究專業知識的概率系統，而其他研究人員則對其準備情況持較樂觀的態度，儘管缺乏具體證據。了解 AI 評審者擅長的地方、短板以及仍面臨的挑戰至關重要。然則，現有的 AI 評審者評估主要集中在其判決是否與人類判決相符（例如，分數一致性、接受預測），這不足以表徵其能力和限制。在本文中，我們通過一項大規模專家標註研究填補了這一空白，在該研究中，45 位物理、生物和健康科學領域的科學家花費 469 小時對 82 篇 Nature 系列論文中人類撰寫和 AI 生成的 2,960 條個別批評（每條針對論文的特定方面）進行評分，評估其正確性、重要性和證據的充分性。在所有三個維度的綜合評分中，由 GPT-5.2 驅動的評審代理的得分高於每篇論文的最高人類評審（60.0% 對 48.2%，p = 0.009），而所有三位 AI 評審者（包括 Gemini 3.0 Pro 和 Claude Opus 4.5）在每個維度上都超過了最低評分的人類。AI 評審者的準確批評也更常被評為重要且證據充分，並提出了 26% 的人類未提及的獨特問題。然而，AI 評審者的重疊程度遠超過人類（交叉評審者對的重疊率為 21% 對 3%），並表現出 16 種人類不具備的重複性弱點，例如有限的子領域知識、缺乏對多個文件的長期上下文管理，以及對小問題的過度批評。總體而言，我們的結果將當前的 AI 評審者定位為人類評審者的補充，而非替代品。
2605.20668.author: Seungone Kim et.al.
